key_insight: The paper turns short-range-order alloy design into a differentiable optimization problem, enabling direct gradient-based tuning from target SRO to target property.
engineering_relevance: This provides a faster, scalable workflow for designing multi-principal-element alloys with mechanically targeted behavior instead of relying on separate structure search and property screening.
method: Replace random-swap reverse Monte Carlo with continuous occupancy variables optimized by gradient descent.
Add a physics-based correction to keep generated SRO thermodynamically realistic for multi-element alloys.
Use a small neural network to map composition and SRO statistics to mechanical properties, closing the design loop.
evidence_signal: Across nine FCC/BCC alloys, the pipeline reproduced SRO-driven stiffness changes from -20% to +57% and matched three of four cobalt-chromium-nickel target stiffness values within 6%.
watch_item: Accuracy depends on sufficiently large simulation cells; the abstract notes that 108-atom cells can mispredict both the direction and magnitude of SRO-induced property changes.
paper_title: Differentiable inverse design of short-range order in high-entropy alloys: from target sro to target property
